303.play: Some bring the murder'd body, some the murderers:

304.play: Let them not speak a word; the guilt is plain;

305.play: For, by my soul, were there worse end than death,

306.play: That end upon them should be executed.

307.play: TAMORA: Andronicus, I will entreat the king;

308.play: Fear not thy sons; they shall do well enough.

309.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Come, Lucius, come; stay not to talk with them.

1.play: Enter DEMETRIUS and CHIRON with LAVINIA, ravished; her hands cut off, and her tongue cut out

2.play: DEMETRIUS: So, now go tell, an if thy tongue can speak,

3.play: Who 'twas that cut thy tongue and ravish'd thee.

4.play: CHIRON: Write down thy mind, bewray thy meaning so,

5.play: An if thy stumps will let thee play the scribe.

6.play: DEMETRIUS: See, how with signs and tokens she can scrowl.

7.play: CHIRON: Go home, call for sweet water, wash thy hands.

8.play: DEMETRIUS: She hath no tongue to call, nor hands to wash;

9.play: And so let's leave her to her silent walks.

10.play: CHIRON: An 'twere my case, I should go hang myself.

11.play: DEMETRIUS: If thou hadst hands to help thee knit the cord.

12.play: MARCUS: Who is this? my niece, that flies away so fast!

13.play: Cousin, a word; where is your husband?

14.play: If I do dream, would all my wealth would wake me!

15.play: If I do wake, some planet strike me down,

16.play: That I may slumber in eternal sleep!

17.play: Speak, gentle niece, what stern ungentle hands

18.play: Have lopp'd and hew'd and made thy body bare

19.play: Of her two branches, those sweet ornaments,

20.play: Whose circling shadows kings have sought to sleep in,

21.play: And might not gain so great a happiness

22.play: As have thy love? Why dost not speak to me?

23.play: Alas, a crimson river of warm blood,

24.play: Like to a bubbling fountain stirr'd with wind,

25.play: Doth rise and fall between thy rosed lips,

26.play: Coming and going with thy honey breath.

27.play: But, sure, some Tereus hath deflowered thee,

28.play: And, lest thou shouldst detect him, cut thy tongue.

29.play: Ah, now thou turn'st away thy face for shame!

30.play: And, notwithstanding all this loss of blood,

31.play: As from a conduit with three issuing spouts,

32.play: Yet do thy cheeks look red as Titan's face

33.play: Blushing to be encountered with a cloud.

34.play: Shall I speak for thee? shall I say 'tis so?

35.play: O, that I knew thy heart; and knew the beast,

36.play: That I might rail at him, to ease my mind!

37.play: Sorrow concealed, like an oven stopp'd,

38.play: Doth burn the heart to cinders where it is.

39.play: Fair Philomela, she but lost her tongue,

40.play: And in a tedious sampler sew'd her mind:

41.play: But, lovely niece, that mean is cut from thee;

42.play: A craftier Tereus, cousin, hast thou met,

43.play: And he hath cut those pretty fingers off,

44.play: That could have better sew'd than Philomel.

45.play: O, had the monster seen those lily hands

46.play: Tremble, like aspen-leaves, upon a lute,

47.play: And make the silken strings delight to kiss them,

48.play: He would not then have touch'd them for his life!

49.play: Or, had he heard the heavenly harmony

50.play: Which that sweet tongue hath made,

51.play: He would have dropp'd his knife, and fell asleep

52.play: As Cerberus at the Thracian poet's feet.

53.play: Come, let us go, and make thy father blind;

54.play: For such a sight will blind a father's eye:

55.play: One hour's storm will drown the fragrant meads;

56.play: What will whole months of tears thy father's eyes?

57.play: Do not draw back, for we will mourn with thee

58.play: O, could our mourning ease thy misery!

1.play: Enter Judges, Senators and Tribunes, with MARTIUS  and QUINTUS, bound, passing on to the place of execution; TITUS going before, pleading

2.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Hear me, grave fathers! noble tribunes, stay!

3.play: For pity of mine age, whose youth was spent

4.play: In dangerous wars, whilst you securely slept;

5.play: For all my blood in Rome's great quarrel shed;

6.play: For all the frosty nights that I have watch'd;

7.play: And for these bitter tears, which now you see

8.play: Filling the aged wrinkles in my cheeks;

9.play: Be pitiful to my condemned sons,

10.play: Whose souls are not corrupted as 'tis thought.

11.play: For two and twenty sons I never wept,

12.play: Because they died in honour's lofty bed.

13.play: For these, these, tribunes, in the dust I write

14.play: My heart's deep languor and my soul's sad tears:

15.play: Let my tears stanch the earth's dry appetite;

16.play: My sons' sweet blood will make it shame and blush.

17.play: O earth, I will befriend thee more with rain,

18.play: That shall distil from these two ancient urns,

19.play: Than youthful April shall with all his showers:

20.play: In summer's drought I'll drop upon thee still;

21.play: In winter with warm tears I'll melt the snow

22.play: And keep eternal spring-time on thy face,

23.play: So thou refuse to drink my dear sons' blood.

24.play: O reverend tribunes! O gentle, aged men!

25.play: Unbind my sons, reverse the doom of death;

26.play: And let me say, that never wept before,

27.play: My tears are now prevailing orators.

28.play: LUCIUS: O noble father, you lament in vain:

29.play: The tribunes hear you not; no man is by;

30.play: And you recount your sorrows to a stone.

31.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Ah, Lucius, for thy brothers let me plead.

32.play: Grave tribunes, once more I entreat of you,--

33.play: LUCIUS: My gracious lord, no tribune hears you speak.

34.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Why, tis no matter, man; if they did hear,

35.play: They would not mark me, or if they did mark,

36.play: They would not pity me, yet plead I must;

37.play: Therefore I tell my sorrows to the stones;

38.play: Who, though they cannot answer my distress,

39.play: Yet in some sort they are better than the tribunes,

40.play: For that they will not intercept my tale:

41.play: When I do weep, they humbly at my feet

42.play: Receive my tears and seem to weep with me;

43.play: And, were they but attired in grave weeds,

44.play: Rome could afford no tribune like to these.

45.play: A stone is soft as wax,--tribunes more hard than stones;

46.play: A stone is silent, and offendeth not,

47.play: And tribunes with their tongues doom men to death.

48.play: But wherefore stand'st thou with thy weapon drawn?

49.play: LUCIUS: To rescue my two brothers from their death:

50.play: For which attempt the judges have pronounced

52.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: O happy man! they have befriended thee.

53.play: Why, foolish Lucius, dost thou not perceive

54.play: That Rome is but a wilderness of tigers?

55.play: Tigers must prey, and Rome affords no prey

56.play: But me and mine: how happy art thou, then,

57.play: From these devourers to be banished!

58.play: But who comes with our brother Marcus here?

59.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Titus, prepare thy aged eyes to weep;

60.play: Or, if not so, thy noble heart to break:

61.play: I bring consuming sorrow to thine age.

62.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Will it consume me? let me see it, then.

63.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: This was thy daughter.

64.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Why, Marcus, so she is.

65.play: LUCIUS: Ay me, this object kills me!

66.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Faint-hearted boy, arise, and look upon her.

68.play: Hath made thee handless in thy father's sight?

69.play: What fool hath added water to the sea,

70.play: Or brought a faggot to bright-burning Troy?

71.play: My grief was at the height before thou camest,

72.play: And now like Nilus, it disdaineth bounds.

73.play: Give me a sword, I'll chop off my hands too;

74.play: For they have fought for Rome, and all in vain;

75.play: And they have nursed this woe, in feeding life;

76.play: In bootless prayer have they been held up,

77.play: And they have served me to effectless use:

78.play: Now all the service I require of them

79.play: Is that the one will help to cut the other.

80.play: 'Tis well, Lavinia, that thou hast no hands;

81.play: For hands, to do Rome service, are but vain.

82.play: LUCIUS: Speak, gentle sister, who hath martyr'd thee?

83.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: O, that delightful engine of her thoughts

84.play: That blabb'd them with such pleasing eloquence,

85.play: Is torn from forth that pretty hollow cage,

86.play: Where, like a sweet melodious bird, it sung

87.play: Sweet varied notes, enchanting every ear!

88.play: LUCIUS: O, say thou for her, who hath done this deed?

89.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: O, thus I found her, straying in the park,

90.play: Seeking to hide herself, as doth the deer

91.play: That hath received some unrecuring wound.

92.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: It was my deer; and he that wounded her

93.play: Hath hurt me more than had he killed me dead:

94.play: For now I stand as one upon a rock

95.play: Environed with a wilderness of sea,

96.play: Who marks the waxing tide grow wave by wave,

97.play: Expecting ever when some envious surge

98.play: Will in his brinish bowels swallow him.

99.play: This way to death my wretched sons are gone;

100.play: Here stands my other son, a banished man,

101.play: And here my brother, weeping at my woes.

102.play: But that which gives my soul the greatest spurn,

103.play: Is dear Lavinia, dearer than my soul.

104.play: Had I but seen thy picture in this plight,

105.play: It would have madded me: what shall I do

106.play: Now I behold thy lively body so?

107.play: Thou hast no hands, to wipe away thy tears:

108.play: Nor tongue, to tell me who hath martyr'd thee:

109.play: Thy husband he is dead: and for his death

110.play: Thy brothers are condemn'd, and dead by this.

111.play: Look, Marcus! ah, son Lucius, look on her!

112.play: When I did name her brothers, then fresh tears

113.play: Stood on her cheeks, as doth the honey-dew

114.play: Upon a gather'd lily almost wither'd.

115.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Perchance she weeps because they kill'd her husband;

116.play: Perchance because she knows them innocent.

117.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: If they did kill thy husband, then be joyful

118.play: Because the law hath ta'en revenge on them.

119.play: No, no, they would not do so foul a deed;

120.play: Witness the sorrow that their sister makes.

121.play: Gentle Lavinia, let me kiss thy lips.

122.play: Or make some sign how I may do thee ease:

123.play: Shall thy good uncle, and thy brother Lucius,

124.play: And thou, and I, sit round about some fountain,

125.play: Looking all downwards to behold our cheeks

126.play: How they are stain'd, as meadows, yet not dry,

127.play: With miry slime left on them by a flood?

128.play: And in the fountain shall we gaze so long

129.play: Till the fresh taste be taken from that clearness,

130.play: And made a brine-pit with our bitter tears?

131.play: Or shall we cut away our hands, like thine?

132.play: Or shall we bite our tongues, and in dumb shows

133.play: Pass the remainder of our hateful days?

134.play: What shall we do? let us, that have our tongues,

135.play: Plot some deuce of further misery,

136.play: To make us wonder'd at in time to come.

137.play: LUCIUS: Sweet father, cease your tears; for, at your grief,

138.play: See how my wretched sister sobs and weeps.

139.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Patience, dear niece. Good Titus, dry thine eyes.

140.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Ah, Marcus, Marcus! brother, well I wot

141.play: Thy napkin cannot drink a tear of mine,

142.play: For thou, poor man, hast drown'd it with thine own.

143.play: LUCIUS: Ah, my Lavinia, I will wipe thy cheeks.

144.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Mark, Marcus, mark! I understand her signs:

145.play: Had she a tongue to speak, now would she say

146.play: That to her brother which I said to thee:

147.play: His napkin, with his true tears all bewet,

148.play: Can do no service on her sorrowful cheeks.

149.play: O, what a sympathy of woe is this,

150.play: As far from help as Limbo is from bliss!

151.play: AARON: Titus Andronicus, my lord the emperor

152.play: Sends thee this word,--that, if thou love thy sons,

153.play: Let Marcus, Lucius, or thyself, old Titus,

154.play: Or any one of you, chop off your hand,

155.play: And send it to the king: he for the same

156.play: Will send thee hither both thy sons alive;

157.play: And that shall be the ransom for their fault.

158.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: O gracious emperor! O gentle Aaron!

159.play: Did ever raven sing so like a lark,

160.play: That gives sweet tidings of the sun's uprise?

161.play: With all my heart, I'll send the emperor My hand:

162.play: Good Aaron, wilt thou help to chop it off?

163.play: LUCIUS: Stay, father! for that noble hand of thine,

164.play: That hath thrown down so many enemies,

165.play: Shall not be sent: my hand will serve the turn:

166.play: My youth can better spare my blood than you;

167.play: And therefore mine shall save my brothers' lives.

168.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Which of your hands hath not defended Rome,

169.play: And rear'd aloft the bloody battle-axe,

170.play: Writing destruction on the enemy's castle?

171.play: O, none of both but are of high desert:

172.play: My hand hath been but idle; let it serve

173.play: To ransom my two nephews from their death;

174.play: Then have I kept it to a worthy end.

175.play: AARON: Nay, come, agree whose hand shall go along,

176.play: For fear they die before their pardon come.

177.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: My hand shall go.

178.play: LUCIUS: By heaven, it shall not go!

179.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Sirs, strive no more: such wither'd herbs as these

180.play: Are meet for plucking up, and therefore mine.

181.play: LUCIUS: Sweet father, if I shall be thought thy son,

182.play: Let me redeem my brothers both from death.

183.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: And, for our father's sake and mother's care,

184.play: Now let me show a brother's love to thee.

185.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Agree between you; I will spare my hand.

186.play: LUCIUS: Then I'll go fetch an axe.

187.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: But I will use the axe.

188.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Come hither, Aaron; I'll deceive them both:

189.play: Lend me thy hand, and I will give thee mine.

190.play: AARON: [Aside]  If that be call'd deceit, I will be honest,

191.play: And never, whilst I live, deceive men so:

192.play: But I'll deceive you in another sort,

193.play: And that you'll say, ere half an hour pass.

194.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Now stay your strife: what shall be is dispatch'd.

195.play: Good Aaron, give his majesty my hand:

196.play: Tell him it was a hand that warded him

197.play: From thousand dangers; bid him bury it

198.play: More hath it merited; that let it have.

199.play: As for my sons, say I account of them

200.play: As jewels purchased at an easy price;

201.play: And yet dear too, because I bought mine own.

202.play: AARON: I go, Andronicus: and for thy hand

203.play: Look by and by to have thy sons with thee.

204.play: Their heads, I mean. O, how this villany

205.play: Doth fat me with the very thoughts of it!

206.play: Let fools do good, and fair men call for grace.

207.play: Aaron will have his soul black like his face.

208.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: O, here I lift this one hand up to heaven,

209.play: And bow this feeble ruin to the earth:

210.play: If any power pities wretched tears,

212.play: What, wilt thou kneel with me?

213.play: Do, then, dear heart; for heaven shall hear our prayers;

214.play: Or with our sighs we'll breathe the welkin dim,

215.play: And stain the sun with fog, as sometime clouds

216.play: When they do hug him in their melting bosoms.

217.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: O brother, speak with possibilities,

218.play: And do not break into these deep extremes.

219.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Is not my sorrow deep, having no bottom?

220.play: Then be my passions bottomless with them.

221.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: But yet let reason govern thy lament.

222.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: If there were reason for these miseries,

223.play: Then into limits could I bind my woes:

224.play: When heaven doth weep, doth not the earth o'erflow?

225.play: If the winds rage, doth not the sea wax mad,

226.play: Threatening the welkin with his big-swoln face?

227.play: And wilt thou have a reason for this coil?

228.play: I am the sea; hark, how her sighs do blow!

229.play: She is the weeping welkin, I the earth:

230.play: Then must my sea be moved with her sighs;

231.play: Then must my earth with her continual tears

232.play: Become a deluge, overflow'd and drown'd;

233.play: For why my bowels cannot hide her woes,

234.play: But like a drunkard must I vomit them.

235.play: Then give me leave, for losers will have leave

236.play: To ease their stomachs with their bitter tongues.

238.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Now let hot AEtna cool in Sicily,

239.play: And be my heart an ever-burning hell!

240.play: These miseries are more than may be borne.

241.play: To weep with them that weep doth ease some deal;

242.play: But sorrow flouted at is double death.

243.play: LUCIUS: Ah, that this sight should make so deep a wound,

244.play: And yet detested life not shrink thereat!

245.play: That ever death should let life bear his name,

246.play: Where life hath no more interest but to breathe!

247.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Alas, poor heart, that kiss is comfortless

248.play: As frozen water to a starved snake.

249.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: When will this fearful slumber have an end?

250.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Now, farewell, flattery: die, Andronicus;

251.play: Thou dost not slumber: see, thy two sons' heads,

252.play: Thy warlike hand, thy mangled daughter here:

253.play: Thy other banish'd son, with this dear sight

254.play: Struck pale and bloodless; and thy brother, I,

255.play: Even like a stony image, cold and numb.

256.play: Ah, now no more will I control thy griefs:

257.play: Rend off thy silver hair, thy other hand

258.play: Gnawing with thy teeth; and be this dismal sight

259.play: The closing up of our most wretched eyes;

260.play: Now is a time to storm; why art thou still?

262.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Why dost thou laugh? it fits not with this hour.

263.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Why, I have not another tear to shed:

264.play: Besides, this sorrow is an enemy,

265.play: And would usurp upon my watery eyes

266.play: And make them blind with tributary tears:

267.play: Then which way shall I find Revenge's cave?

268.play: For these two heads do seem to speak to me,

269.play: And threat me I shall never come to bliss

270.play: Till all these mischiefs be return'd again

271.play: Even in their throats that have committed them.

272.play: Come, let me see what task I have to do.

273.play: You heavy people, circle me about,

274.play: That I may turn me to each one of you,

275.play: And swear unto my soul to right your wrongs.

276.play: The vow is made. Come, brother, take a head;

277.play: And in this hand the other I will bear.

278.play: Lavinia, thou shalt be employ'd: these arms!

279.play: Bear thou my hand, sweet wench, between thy teeth.

280.play: As for thee, boy, go get thee from my sight;

281.play: Thou art an exile, and thou must not stay:

282.play: Hie to the Goths, and raise an army there:

283.play: And, if you love me, as I think you do,

284.play: Let's kiss and part, for we have much to do.

285.play: LUCIUS: Farewell Andronicus, my noble father,

286.play: The wofull'st man that ever lived in Rome:

287.play: Farewell, proud Rome; till Lucius come again,

288.play: He leaves his pledges dearer than his life:

290.play: O, would thou wert as thou tofore hast been!

291.play: But now nor Lucius nor Lavinia lives

292.play: But in oblivion and hateful griefs.

293.play: If Lucius live, he will requite your wrongs;

294.play: And make proud Saturnine and his empress

295.play: Beg at the gates, like Tarquin and his queen.

296.play: Now will I to the Goths, and raise a power,

297.play: To be revenged on Rome and Saturnine.

1.play: Enter TITUS, MARCUS, LAVINIA and Young LUCIUS, a boy

2.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: So, so; now sit: and look you eat no more

3.play: Than will preserve just so much strength in us

4.play: As will revenge these bitter woes of ours.

6.play: Thy niece and I, poor creatures, want our hands,

7.play: And cannot passionate our tenfold grief

8.play: With folded arms. This poor right hand of mine

9.play: Is left to tyrannize upon my breast;

10.play: Who, when my heart, all mad with misery,

11.play: Beats in this hollow prison of my flesh,

12.play: Then thus I thump it down.

13.play: Thou map of woe, that thus dost talk in signs!

14.play: When thy poor heart beats with outrageous beating,

15.play: Thou canst not strike it thus to make it still.

16.play: Wound it with sighing, girl, kill it with groans;

17.play: Or get some little knife between thy teeth,

18.play: And just against thy heart make thou a hole;

19.play: That all the tears that thy poor eyes let fall

20.play: May run into that sink, and soaking in

21.play: Drown the lamenting fool in sea-salt tears.

22.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Fie, brother, fie! teach her not thus to lay

23.play: Such violent hands upon her tender life.

24.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: How now! has sorrow made thee dote already?

25.play: Why, Marcus, no man should be mad but I.

26.play: What violent hands can she lay on her life?

27.play: Ah, wherefore dost thou urge the name of hands;

28.play: To bid AEneas tell the tale twice o'er,

29.play: How Troy was burnt and he made miserable?

30.play: O, handle not the theme, to talk of hands,

31.play: Lest we remember still that we have none.

32.play: Fie, fie, how franticly I square my talk,

33.play: As if we should forget we had no hands,

34.play: If Marcus did not name the word of hands!

35.play: Come, let's fall to; and, gentle girl, eat this:

36.play: Here is no drink! Hark, Marcus, what she says;

37.play: I can interpret all her martyr'd signs;

38.play: She says she drinks no other drink but tears,

39.play: Brew'd with her sorrow, mesh'd upon her cheeks:

40.play: Speechless complainer, I will learn thy thought;

41.play: In thy dumb action will I be as perfect

42.play: As begging hermits in their holy prayers:

43.play: Thou shalt not sigh, nor hold thy stumps to heaven,

44.play: Nor wink, nor nod, nor kneel, nor make a sign,

45.play: But I of these will wrest an alphabet

46.play: And by still practise learn to know thy meaning.

47.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Alas, the tender boy, in passion moved,

48.play: Doth weep to see his grandsire's heaviness.

49.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Peace, tender sapling; thou art made of tears,

50.play: And tears will quickly melt thy life away.

51.play: What dost thou strike at, Marcus, with thy knife?

52.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: At that that I have kill'd, my lord; a fly.

53.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Out on thee, murderer! thou kill'st my heart;

54.play: Mine eyes are cloy'd with view of tyranny:

55.play: A deed of death done on the innocent

56.play: Becomes not Titus' brother: get thee gone:

57.play: I see thou art not for my company.

58.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Alas, my lord, I have but kill'd a fly.

59.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: But how, if that fly had a father and mother?

60.play: How would he hang his slender gilded wings,

61.play: And buzz lamenting doings in the air!

63.play: That, with his pretty buzzing melody,

64.play: Came here to make us merry! and thou hast

66.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Pardon me, sir; it was a black ill-favor'd fly,

67.play: Like to the empress' Moor; therefore I kill'd him.

69.play: Then pardon me for reprehending thee,

70.play: For thou hast done a charitable deed.

71.play: Give me thy knife, I will insult on him;

72.play: Flattering myself, as if it were the Moor

73.play: Come hither purposely to poison me.--

74.play: There's for thyself, and that's for Tamora.

76.play: Yet, I think, we are not brought so low,

77.play: But that between us we can kill a fly

78.play: That comes in likeness of a coal-black Moor.

79.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Alas, poor man! grief has so wrought on him,

80.play: He takes false shadows for true substances.

81.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Come, take away. Lavinia, go with me:

82.play: I'll to thy closet; and go read with thee

83.play: Sad stories chanced in the times of old.

84.play: Come, boy, and go with me: thy sight is young,

85.play: And thou shalt read when mine begin to dazzle.

1.play: Enter young LUCIUS, and LAVINIA running after him,  and the boy flies from her, with books under his arm. Then enter TITUS and MARCUS

2.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Stand by me, Lucius; do not fear thine aunt.

3.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: She loves thee, boy, too well to do thee harm.

4.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: What means my niece Lavinia by these signs?

5.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Fear her not, Lucius: somewhat doth she mean:

6.play: See, Lucius, see how much she makes of thee:

109.play: SATURNINUS: Then go successantly, and plead to him.

1.play: Enter LUCIUS with an army of Goths, with drum and colours

2.play: LUCIUS: Approved warriors, and my faithful friends,

3.play: I have received letters from great Rome,

4.play: Which signify what hate they bear their emperor

5.play: And how desirous of our sight they are.

6.play: Therefore, great lords, be, as your titles witness,

7.play: Imperious and impatient of your wrongs,

8.play: And wherein Rome hath done you any scath,

10.play: I humbly thank him, and I thank you all.

11.play: But who comes here, led by a lusty Goth?

12.play: O worthy Goth, this is the incarnate devil

13.play: That robb'd Andronicus of his good hand;

14.play: This is the pearl that pleased your empress' eye,

15.play: And here's the base fruit of his burning lust.

16.play: Say, wall-eyed slave, whither wouldst thou convey

17.play: This growing image of thy fiend-like face?

18.play: Why dost not speak? what, deaf? not a word?

19.play: A halter, soldiers! hang him on this tree.

20.play: And by his side his fruit of bastardy.

21.play: AARON: Touch not the boy; he is of royal blood.

22.play: LUCIUS: Too like the sire for ever being good.

23.play: First hang the child, that he may see it sprawl;

24.play: A sight to vex the father's soul withal.

27.play: And bear it from me to the empress.

28.play: If thou do this, I'll show thee wondrous things,

29.play: That highly may advantage thee to hear:

30.play: If thou wilt not, befall what may befall,

31.play: I'll speak no more but 'Vengeance rot you all!'

32.play: LUCIUS: Say on: an if it please me which thou speak'st

33.play: Thy child shall live, and I will see it nourish'd.

34.play: AARON: An if it please thee! why, assure thee, Lucius,

35.play: 'Twill vex thy soul to hear what I shall speak;

36.play: For I must talk of murders, rapes and massacres,

37.play: Acts of black night, abominable deeds,

39.play: Ruthful to hear, yet piteously perform'd:

40.play: And this shall all be buried by my death,

41.play: Unless thou swear to me my child shall live.

42.play: LUCIUS: Tell on thy mind; I say thy child shall live.

43.play: AARON: Swear that he shall, and then I will begin.

44.play: LUCIUS: Who should I swear by? thou believest no god:

45.play: That granted, how canst thou believe an oath?

46.play: AARON: What if I do not? as, indeed, I do not;

47.play: Yet, for I know thou art religious

48.play: And hast a thing within thee called conscience,

49.play: With twenty popish tricks and ceremonies,

50.play: Which I have seen thee careful to observe,

51.play: Therefore I urge thy oath; for that I know

52.play: An idiot holds his bauble for a god

53.play: And keeps the oath which by that god he swears,

54.play: To that I'll urge him: therefore thou shalt vow

55.play: By that same god, what god soe'er it be,

56.play: That thou adorest and hast in reverence,

57.play: To save my boy, to nourish and bring him up;

58.play: Or else I will discover nought to thee.

59.play: LUCIUS: Even by my god I swear to thee I will.

60.play: AARON: First know thou, I begot him on the empress.

61.play: LUCIUS: O most insatiate and luxurious woman!

62.play: AARON: Tut, Lucius, this was but a deed of charity

63.play: To that which thou shalt hear of me anon.

64.play: 'Twas her two sons that murder'd Bassianus;

65.play: They cut thy sister's tongue and ravish'd her

66.play: And cut her hands and trimm'd her as thou saw'st.

67.play: LUCIUS: O detestable villain! call'st thou that trimming?

68.play: AARON: Why, she was wash'd and cut and trimm'd, and 'twas

69.play: Trim sport for them that had the doing of it.

70.play: LUCIUS: O barbarous, beastly villains, like thyself!

71.play: AARON: Indeed, I was their tutor to instruct them:

72.play: That codding spirit had they from their mother,

73.play: As sure a card as ever won the set;

74.play: That bloody mind, I think, they learn'd of me,

75.play: As true a dog as ever fought at head.

76.play: Well, let my deeds be witness of my worth.

77.play: I train'd thy brethren to that guileful hole

78.play: Where the dead corpse of Bassianus lay:

79.play: I wrote the letter that thy father found

80.play: And hid the gold within the letter mention'd,

81.play: Confederate with the queen and her two sons:

82.play: And what not done, that thou hast cause to rue,

83.play: Wherein I had no stroke of mischief in it?

84.play: I play'd the cheater for thy father's hand,

85.play: And, when I had it, drew myself apart

86.play: And almost broke my heart with extreme laughter:

87.play: I pry'd me through the crevice of a wall

88.play: When, for his hand, he had his two sons' heads;

89.play: Beheld his tears, and laugh'd so heartily,

90.play: That both mine eyes were rainy like to his :

91.play: And when I told the empress of this sport,

92.play: She swooned almost at my pleasing tale,

93.play: And for my tidings gave me twenty kisses.

94.play: Ay, like a black dog, as the saying is.

95.play: LUCIUS: Art thou not sorry for these heinous deeds?

96.play: AARON: Ay, that I had not done a thousand more.

97.play: Even now I curse the day--and yet, I think,

98.play: Few come within the compass of my curse,--

99.play: Wherein I did not some notorious ill,

100.play: As kill a man, or else devise his death,

101.play: Ravish a maid, or plot the way to do it,

102.play: Accuse some innocent and forswear myself,

103.play: Set deadly enmity between two friends,

104.play: Make poor men's cattle break their necks;

105.play: Set fire on barns and hay-stacks in the night,

106.play: And bid the owners quench them with their tears.

107.play: Oft have I digg'd up dead men from their graves,

108.play: And set them upright at their dear friends' doors,

109.play: Even when their sorrows almost were forgot;

110.play: And on their skins, as on the bark of trees,

111.play: Have with my knife carved in Roman letters,

112.play: 'Let not your sorrow die, though I am dead.'

113.play: Tut, I have done a thousand dreadful things

114.play: As willingly as one would kill a fly,

115.play: And nothing grieves me heartily indeed

116.play: But that I cannot do ten thousand more.

117.play: LUCIUS: Bring down the devil; for he must not die

118.play: So sweet a death as hanging presently.

119.play: AARON: If there be devils, would I were a devil,

120.play: To live and burn in everlasting fire,

121.play: So I might have your company in hell,

122.play: But to torment you with my bitter tongue!

123.play: LUCIUS: Sirs, stop his mouth, and let him speak no more.

125.play: Welcome, AEmilius	what's the news from Rome?

126.play: AEMILIUS: Lord Lucius, and you princes of the Goths,

127.play: The Roman emperor greets you all by me;

128.play: And, for he understands you are in arms,

129.play: He craves a parley at your father's house,

130.play: Willing you to demand your hostages,

131.play: And they shall be immediately deliver'd.

132.play: LUCIUS: AEmilius, let the emperor give his pledges

133.play: Unto my father and my uncle Marcus,

134.play: And we will come. March away.

1.play: Enter TAMORA, DEMETRIUS, and CHIRON, disguised

2.play: TAMORA: Thus, in this strange and sad habiliment,

4.play: And say I am Revenge, sent from below

5.play: To join with him and right his heinous wrongs.

6.play: Knock at his study, where, they say, he keeps,

7.play: To ruminate strange plots of dire revenge;

8.play: Tell him Revenge is come to join with him,

9.play: And work confusion on his enemies.

10.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Who doth molest my contemplation?

11.play: Is it your trick to make me ope the door,

12.play: That so my sad decrees may fly away,

13.play: And all my study be to no effect?

14.play: You are deceived: for what I mean to do

15.play: See here in bloody lines I have set down;

16.play: And what is written shall be executed.

17.play: TAMORA: Titus, I am come to talk with thee.

18.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: No, not a word; how can I grace my talk,

19.play: Wanting a hand to give it action?

20.play: Thou hast the odds of me; therefore no more.

21.play: TAMORA: If thou didst know me, thou wouldest talk with me.

22.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: I am not mad; I know thee well enough:

23.play: Witness this wretched stump, witness these crimson lines;

24.play: Witness these trenches made by grief and care,

25.play: Witness the tiring day and heavy night;

26.play: Witness all sorrow, that I know thee well

27.play: For our proud empress, mighty Tamora:

28.play: Is not thy coming for my other hand?

29.play: TAMORA: Know, thou sad man, I am not Tamora;

30.play: She is thy enemy, and I thy friend:

31.play: I am Revenge: sent from the infernal kingdom,

32.play: To ease the gnawing vulture of thy mind,

33.play: By working wreakful vengeance on thy foes.

34.play: Come down, and welcome me to this world's light;

35.play: Confer with me of murder and of death:

36.play: There's not a hollow cave or lurking-place,

37.play: No vast obscurity or misty vale,

38.play: Where bloody murder or detested rape

39.play: Can couch for fear, but I will find them out;

40.play: And in their ears tell them my dreadful name,

41.play: Revenge, which makes the foul offender quake.

42.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Art thou Revenge? and art thou sent to me,

43.play: To be a torment to mine enemies?

44.play: TAMORA: I am; therefore come down, and welcome me.

45.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Do me some service, ere I come to thee.

46.play: Lo, by thy side where Rape and Murder stands;

47.play: Now give me some surance that thou art Revenge,

48.play: Stab them, or tear them on thy chariot-wheels;

49.play: And then I'll come and be thy waggoner,

50.play: And whirl along with thee about the globe.

51.play: Provide thee two proper palfreys, black as jet,

52.play: To hale thy vengeful waggon swift away,

53.play: And find out murderers in their guilty caves:

54.play: And when thy car is loaden with their heads,

55.play: I will dismount, and by the waggon-wheel

56.play: Trot, like a servile footman, all day long,

57.play: Even from Hyperion's rising in the east

58.play: Until his very downfall in the sea:

59.play: And day by day I'll do this heavy task,

60.play: So thou destroy Rapine and Murder there.

61.play: TAMORA: These are my ministers, and come with me.

62.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Are these thy ministers? what are they call'd?

63.play: TAMORA: Rapine and Murder; therefore called so,

64.play: Cause they take vengeance of such kind of men.

65.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Good Lord, how like the empress' sons they are!

66.play: And you, the empress! but we worldly men

68.play: O sweet Revenge, now do I come to thee;

69.play: And, if one arm's embracement will content thee,

70.play: I will embrace thee in it by and by.

71.play: TAMORA: This closing with him fits his lunacy

72.play: Whate'er I forge to feed his brain-sick fits,

73.play: Do you uphold and maintain in your speeches,

74.play: For now he firmly takes me for Revenge;

75.play: And, being credulous in this mad thought,

76.play: I'll make him send for Lucius his son;

77.play: And, whilst I at a banquet hold him sure,

78.play: I'll find some cunning practise out of hand,

79.play: To scatter and disperse the giddy Goths,

80.play: Or, at the least, make them his enemies.

81.play: See, here he comes, and I must ply my theme.

82.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Long have I been forlorn, and all for thee:

83.play: Welcome, dread Fury, to my woful house:

84.play: Rapine and Murder, you are welcome too.

85.play: How like the empress and her sons you are!

86.play: Well are you fitted, had you but a Moor:

87.play: Could not all hell afford you such a devil?

88.play: For well I wot the empress never wags

89.play: But in her company there is a Moor;

90.play: And, would you represent our queen aright,

91.play: It were convenient you had such a devil:

92.play: But welcome, as you are. What shall we do?

93.play: TAMORA: What wouldst thou have us do, Andronicus?

94.play: DEMETRIUS: Show me a murderer, I'll deal with him.

95.play: CHIRON: Show me a villain that hath done a rape,

96.play: And I am sent to be revenged on him.

97.play: TAMORA: Show me a thousand that have done thee wrong,

98.play: And I will be revenged on them all.

99.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Look round about the wicked streets of Rome;

100.play: And when thou find'st a man that's like thyself.

101.play: Good Murder, stab him; he's a murderer.

102.play: Go thou with him; and when it is thy hap

103.play: To find another that is like to thee,

104.play: Good Rapine, stab him; he's a ravisher.

105.play: Go thou with them; and in the emperor's court

106.play: There is a queen, attended by a Moor;

107.play: Well mayst thou know her by thy own proportion,

108.play: for up and down she doth resemble thee:

109.play: I pray thee, do on them some violent death;

110.play: They have been violent to me and mine.

111.play: TAMORA: Well hast thou lesson'd us; this shall we do.

112.play: But would it please thee, good Andronicus,

113.play: To send for Lucius, thy thrice-valiant son,

114.play: Who leads towards Rome a band of warlike Goths,

115.play: And bid him come and banquet at thy house;

116.play: When he is here, even at thy solemn feast,

117.play: I will bring in the empress and her sons,

118.play: The emperor himself and all thy foes;

119.play: And at thy mercy shalt they stoop and kneel,

120.play: And on them shalt thou ease thy angry heart.

121.play: What says Andronicus to this device?

122.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Marcus, my brother! 'tis sad Titus calls.

123.play: Go, gentle Marcus, to thy nephew Lucius;

124.play: Thou shalt inquire him out among the Goths:

125.play: Bid him repair to me, and bring with him

126.play: Some of the chiefest princes of the Goths;

127.play: Bid him encamp his soldiers where they are:

128.play: Tell him the emperor and the empress too

129.play: Feast at my house, and he shall feast with them.

130.play: This do thou for my love; and so let him,

131.play: As he regards his aged father's life.

132.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: This will I do, and soon return again.

133.play: TAMORA: Now will I hence about thy business,

134.play: And take my ministers along with me.

135.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Nay, nay, let Rape and Murder stay with me;

136.play: Or else I'll call my brother back again,

137.play: And cleave to no revenge but Lucius.

138.play: TAMORA: [Aside to her sons]  What say you, boys? will you

140.play: Whiles I go tell my lord the emperor

141.play: How I have govern'd our determined jest?

142.play: Yield to his humour, smooth and speak him fair,

143.play: And tarry with him till I turn again.

144.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: [Aside]  I know them all, though they suppose me mad,

145.play: And will o'erreach them in their own devices:

146.play: A pair of cursed hell-hounds and their dam!

147.play: DEMETRIUS: Madam, depart at pleasure; leave us here.

148.play: TAMORA: Farewell, Andronicus: Revenge now goes

149.play: To lay a complot to betray thy foes.

150.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: I know thou dost; and, sweet Revenge, farewell.

151.play: CHIRON: Tell us, old man, how shall we be employ'd?

152.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Tut, I have work enough for you to do.

153.play: Publius, come hither, Caius, and Valentine!

155.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Know you these two?

156.play: PUBLIUS: The empress' sons, I take them, Chiron and Demetrius.

157.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Fie, Publius, fie! thou art too much deceived;

158.play: The one is Murder, Rape is the other's name;

159.play: And therefore bind them, gentle Publius.

160.play: Caius and Valentine, lay hands on them.

161.play: Oft have you heard me wish for such an hour,

162.play: And now I find it; therefore bind them sure,

163.play: And stop their mouths, if they begin to cry.

164.play: CHIRON: Villains, forbear! we are the empress' sons.

165.play: PUBLIUS: And therefore do we what we are commanded.

166.play: Stop close their mouths, let them not speak a word.

167.play: Is he sure bound? look that you bind them fast.

168.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Come, come, Lavinia; look, thy foes are bound.

169.play: Sirs, stop their mouths, let them not speak to me;

170.play: But let them hear what fearful words I utter.

172.play: Here stands the spring whom you have stain'd with mud,

173.play: This goodly summer with your winter mix'd.

174.play: You kill'd her husband, and for that vile fault

175.play: Two of her brothers were condemn'd to death,

176.play: My hand cut off and made a merry jest;

177.play: Both her sweet hands, her tongue, and that more dear

178.play: Than hands or tongue, her spotless chastity,

179.play: Inhuman traitors, you constrain'd and forced.

180.play: What would you say, if I should let you speak?

181.play: Villains, for shame you could not beg for grace.

182.play: Hark, wretches! how I mean to martyr you.

183.play: This one hand yet is left to cut your throats,

184.play: Whilst that Lavinia 'tween her stumps doth hold

185.play: The basin that receives your guilty blood.

186.play: You know your mother means to feast with me,

187.play: And calls herself Revenge, and thinks me mad:

188.play: Hark, villains! I will grind your bones to dust

189.play: And with your blood and it I'll make a paste,

190.play: And of the paste a coffin I will rear

191.play: And make two pasties of your shameful heads,

192.play: And bid that strumpet, your unhallow'd dam,

193.play: Like to the earth swallow her own increase.

194.play: This is the feast that I have bid her to,

195.play: And this the banquet she shall surfeit on;

196.play: For worse than Philomel you used my daughter,

197.play: And worse than Progne I will be revenged:

198.play: And now prepare your throats. Lavinia, come,

199.play: Receive the blood: and when that they are dead,

200.play: Let me go grind their bones to powder small

201.play: And with this hateful liquor temper it;

202.play: And in that paste let their vile heads be baked.

203.play: Come, come, be every one officious

204.play: To make this banquet; which I wish may prove

205.play: More stern and bloody than the Centaurs' feast.

206.play: So, now bring them in, for I'll play the cook,

207.play: And see them ready 'gainst their mother comes.

1.play: Enter LUCIUS, MARCUS, and Goths, with AARON prisoner

2.play: LUCIUS: Uncle Marcus, since it is my father's mind

3.play: That I repair to Rome, I am content.

4.play: Good uncle, take you in this barbarous Moor,

5.play: This ravenous tiger, this accursed devil;

6.play: Let him receive no sustenance, fetter him

7.play: Till he be brought unto the empress' face,

8.play: For testimony of her foul proceedings:

9.play: And see the ambush of our friends be strong;

10.play: I fear the emperor means no good to us.

11.play: AARON: Some devil whisper curses in mine ear,

12.play: And prompt me, that my tongue may utter forth

13.play: The venomous malice of my swelling heart!

14.play: LUCIUS: Away, inhuman dog! unhallow'd slave!

15.play: Sirs, help our uncle to convey him in.

16.play: The trumpets show the emperor is at hand.

17.play: SATURNINUS: What, hath the firmament more suns than one?

18.play: LUCIUS: What boots it thee to call thyself a sun?

19.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Rome's emperor, and nephew, break the parle;

20.play: These quarrels must be quietly debated.

21.play: The feast is ready, which the careful Titus

22.play: Hath ordain'd to an honourable end,

23.play: For peace, for love, for league, and good to Rome:

24.play: Please you, therefore, draw nigh, and take your places.

26.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Welcome, my gracious lord; welcome, dread queen;

27.play: Welcome, ye warlike Goths; welcome, Lucius;

28.play: And welcome, all: although the cheer be poor,

29.play: 'Twill fill your stomachs; please you eat of it.

30.play: SATURNINUS: Why art thou thus attired, Andronicus?

31.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Because I would be sure to have all well,

32.play: To entertain your highness and your empress.

33.play: TAMORA: We are beholding to you, good Andronicus.

34.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: An if your highness knew my heart, you were.

35.play: My lord the emperor, resolve me this:

36.play: Was it well done of rash Virginius

37.play: To slay his daughter with his own right hand,

38.play: Because she was enforced, stain'd, and deflower'd?

40.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Your reason, mighty lord?

41.play: SATURNINUS: Because the girl should not survive her shame,

42.play: And by her presence still renew his sorrows.

43.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: A reason mighty, strong, and effectual;

44.play: A pattern, precedent, and lively warrant,

45.play: For me, most wretched, to perform the like.

46.play: Die, die, Lavinia, and thy shame with thee;

47.play: And, with thy shame, thy father's sorrow die!

48.play: SATURNINUS: What hast thou done, unnatural and unkind?

49.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Kill'd her, for whom my tears have made me blind.

50.play: I am as woful as Virginius was,

51.play: And have a thousand times more cause than he

52.play: To do this outrage: and it now is done.

53.play: SATURNINUS: What, was she ravish'd? tell who did the deed.

54.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Will't please you eat? will't please your

56.play: TAMORA: Why hast thou slain thine only daughter thus?

57.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Not I; 'twas Chiron and Demetrius:

58.play: They ravish'd her, and cut away her tongue;

59.play: And they, 'twas they, that did her all this wrong.

60.play: SATURNINUS: Go fetch them hither to us presently.

61.play: TITUS ANDRONICUS: Why, there they are both, baked in that pie;

62.play: Whereof their mother daintily hath fed,

63.play: Eating the flesh that she herself hath bred.

64.play: 'Tis true, 'tis true; witness my knife's sharp point.

65.play: SATURNINUS: Die, frantic wretch, for this accursed deed!

66.play: LUCIUS: Can the son's eye behold his father bleed?

67.play: There's meed for meed, death for a deadly deed!

68.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: You sad-faced men, people and sons of Rome,

69.play: By uproar sever'd, like a flight of fowl

70.play: Scatter'd by winds and high tempestuous gusts,

71.play: O, let me teach you how to knit again

72.play: This scatter'd corn  into one mutual sheaf,

73.play: These broken limbs again into one body;

74.play: Lest Rome herself be bane unto herself,

75.play: And she whom mighty kingdoms court'sy to,

76.play: Like a forlorn and desperate castaway,

78.play: But if my frosty signs and chaps of age,

80.play: Cannot induce you to attend my words,

81.play: Speak, Rome's dear friend, as erst our ancestor,

82.play: When with his solemn tongue he did discourse

83.play: To love-sick Dido's sad attending ear

84.play: The story of that baleful burning night

85.play: When subtle Greeks surprised King Priam's Troy,

86.play: Tell us what Sinon hath bewitch'd our ears,

87.play: Or who hath brought the fatal engine in

88.play: That gives our Troy, our Rome, the civil wound.

89.play: My heart is not compact of flint nor steel;

90.play: Nor can I utter all our bitter grief,

91.play: But floods of tears will drown my oratory,

92.play: And break my utterance, even in the time

93.play: When it should move you to attend me most,

95.play: Here is a captain, let him tell the tale;

96.play: Your hearts will throb and weep to hear him speak.

97.play: LUCIUS: Then, noble auditory, be it known to you,

99.play: Were they that murdered our emperor's brother;

100.play: And they it were that ravished our sister:

101.play: For their fell faults our brothers were beheaded;

102.play: Our father's tears despised, and basely cozen'd

103.play: Of that true hand that fought Rome's quarrel out,

104.play: And sent her enemies unto the grave.

106.play: The gates shut on me, and turn'd weeping out,

107.play: To beg relief among Rome's enemies:

108.play: Who drown'd their enmity in my true tears.

109.play: And oped their arms to embrace me as a friend.

110.play: I am the turned forth, be it known to you,

111.play: That have preserved her welfare in my blood;

112.play: And from her bosom took the enemy's point,

113.play: Sheathing the steel in my adventurous body.

114.play: Alas, you know I am no vaunter, I;

115.play: My scars can witness, dumb although they are,

116.play: That my report is just and full of truth.

117.play: But, soft! methinks I do digress too much,

118.play: Citing my worthless praise: O, pardon me;

119.play: For when no friends are by, men praise themselves.

120.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Now is my turn to speak. Behold this child:

122.play: The issue of an irreligious Moor,

123.play: Chief architect and plotter of these woes:

124.play: The villain is alive in Titus' house,

125.play: And as he is, to witness this is true.

126.play: Now judge what cause had Titus to revenge

128.play: Or more than any living man could bear.

129.play: Now you have heard the truth, what say you, Romans?

130.play: Have we done aught amiss,--show us wherein,

131.play: And, from the place where you behold us now,

133.play: Will, hand in hand, all headlong cast us down.

134.play: And on the ragged stones beat forth our brains,

135.play: And make a mutual closure of our house.

136.play: Speak, Romans, speak; and if you say we shall,

137.play: Lo, hand in hand, Lucius and I will fall.

138.play: AEMILIUS: Come, come, thou reverend man of Rome,

139.play: And bring our emperor gently in thy hand,

140.play: Lucius our emperor; for well I know

141.play: The common voice do cry it shall be so.

142.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Go, go into old Titus' sorrowful house,

143.play: And hither hale that misbelieving Moor,

144.play: To be adjudged some direful slaughtering death,

145.play: As punishment for his most wicked life.

146.play: LUCIUS: Thanks, gentle Romans: may I govern so,

147.play: To heal Rome's harms, and wipe away her woe!

148.play: But, gentle people, give me aim awhile,

149.play: For nature puts me to a heavy task:

150.play: Stand all aloof: but, uncle, draw you near,

151.play: To shed obsequious tears upon this trunk.

152.play: O, take this warm kiss on thy pale cold lips,

153.play: These sorrowful drops upon thy blood-stain'd face,

154.play: The last true duties of thy noble son!

155.play: MARCUS ANDRONICUS: Tear for tear, and loving kiss for kiss,

156.play: Thy brother Marcus tenders on thy lips:

157.play: O were the sum of these that I should pay

158.play: Countless and infinite, yet would I pay them!

159.play: LUCIUS: Come hither, boy; come, come, and learn of us

160.play: To melt in showers: thy grandsire loved thee well:

161.play: Many a time he danced thee on his knee,

162.play: Sung thee asleep, his loving breast thy pillow:

163.play: Many a matter hath he told to thee,

164.play: Meet and agreeing with thine infancy;

165.play: In that respect, then, like a loving child,

166.play: Shed yet some small drops from thy tender spring,

167.play: Because kind nature doth require it so:

168.play: Friends should associate friends in grief and woe:

169.play: Bid him farewell; commit him to the grave;

170.play: Do him that kindness, and take leave of him.

172.play: AEMILIUS: You sad Andronici, have done with woes:

173.play: Give sentence on this execrable wretch,

174.play: That hath been breeder of these dire events.

175.play: LUCIUS: Set him breast-deep in earth, and famish him;

176.play: There let him stand, and rave, and cry for food;

177.play: If any one relieves or pities him,

178.play: For the offence he dies. This is our doom:

179.play: Some stay to see him fasten'd in the earth.

180.play: AARON: O, why should wrath be mute, and fury dumb?

181.play: I am no baby, I, that with base prayers

182.play: I should repent the evils I have done:

183.play: Ten thousand worse than ever yet I did

184.play: Would I perform, if I might have my will;

185.play: If one good deed in all my life I did,

186.play: I do repent it from my very soul.

187.play: LUCIUS: Some loving friends convey the emperor hence,

188.play: And give him burial in his father's grave:

189.play: My father and Lavinia shall forthwith

190.play: Be closed in our household's monument.

191.play: As for that heinous tiger, Tamora,

192.play: No funeral rite, nor man in mourning weeds,

193.play: No mournful bell shall ring her burial;

194.play: But throw her forth to beasts and birds of prey:

195.play: Her life was beast-like, and devoid of pity;

196.play: And, being so, shall have like want of pity.

197.play: See justice done on Aaron, that damn'd Moor,

198.play: By whom our heavy haps had their beginning:

199.play: Then, afterwards, to order well the state,

200.play: That like events may ne'er it ruinate.
